title: Cape Feare

jokes.text: Ladies and gentlemen, it's Up Late With McBain. I'm your announcer, Obergruppenfuehrer Wolfcastle.
jokes.setup: Introduction of a late-night talk show host
jokes.punchline: The announcer has a Nazi military title instead of a normal name
jokes.explanation: Clever subversion mixing wholesome American TV format with fascist imagery, characteristic of Simpsons' dark satire

dialogue.speaker: Announcer
dialogue.line: Ladies and gentlemen, please welcome your host, Oberführer Jay Leno!

jokes.text: That is some outfit, Scoey. It makes you look like a homosexual.
jokes.setup: Host complimenting band leader's outfit
jokes.punchline: Delivers casual homophobic insult as if it's a compliment
jokes.explanation: Character-appropriate clueless offensiveness, but relies on shock value more than clever writing

dialogue.speaker: Host
dialogue.line: That's a fabulous outfit!

dialogue.speaker: McBain
dialogue.line: Thanks. You know, I got it at a really gay store.

jokes.text: This is horrible. The Fox network has sunk to a new low.
jokes.setup: Character criticizing bad television
jokes.punchline: The show is criticizing its own network
jokes.explanation: Brilliant meta-humor where the show bites the hand that feeds it, perfectly economical delivery

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Why you little... (chokes Bart) I'm watching TV here!

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Dad, you're watching Fox.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Woo-hoo, Fox!

jokes.text: Replaced by the benevolent General Krull. All hail Krull and his glorious new regime! Sincerely, Little Girl.
jokes.setup: Lisa's pen pal writing about political upheaval
jokes.punchline: Letter is clearly being dictated by the new dictator, signed 'Little Girl'
jokes.explanation: Expertly constructed dark comedy about authoritarian propaganda, with perfect absurd signature

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: Dear Lisa, everything is wonderful here. The new leader has promised us a bright future.

dialogue.speaker: Dictator
dialogue.line: Write that the old ways were corrupt!

dialogue.speaker: Anya
dialogue.line: The old ways were corrupt!

dialogue.speaker: Dictator
dialogue.line: Write that we must sacrifice for the greater good!

dialogue.speaker: Anya
dialogue.line: We must sacrifice for the greater good!

dialogue.speaker: Dictator
dialogue.line: Sign it 'Little Girl'.

dialogue.speaker: Anya
dialogue.line: With love, Little Girl.

jokes.text: 'I'm going to kill you.'
jokes.setup: After Lisa gets a disturbing letter, Bart also receives mail
jokes.punchline: His letter is direct and threatening
jokes.explanation: Effective escalation from Lisa's complex political letter to Bart's simple threat, good economy

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Ay caramba! What is this?

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Well, what does it say?

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: It says... 'We know what you did. We're coming for you.'

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: Oh my!

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: It's signed... Blockbuster Video.

jokes.text: Oh, uh, I wrote that one... after Bart somehow put this tattoo on my butt.
jokes.setup: Family examining threatening letters in different handwriting
jokes.punchline: Homer admits to writing one, reveals embarrassing tattoo reason
jokes.explanation: Classic Homer character moment - childish revenge and casual TMI sharing

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: I wrote one of these letters.

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: Homer! Why?

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Because I got this tattoo on a dare, and I was so embarrassed that I wanted to scare everyone into moving away so nobody would ever see it.

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: What's the tattoo of?

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: It says 'Mother'.

jokes.text: But who'd want to hurt me? I'm this century's Dennis the Menace.
jokes.setup: Bart wondering who would want to hurt him
jokes.punchline: Compares himself to innocent comic character while being genuinely menacing
jokes.explanation: Perfect character-based irony - Bart's complete lack of self-awareness about his troublemaking

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Who would want to hurt me? I'm like an innocent comic book character.

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Ay caramba!

jokes.text: It's probably the person you least suspect.
jokes.setup: Family discussing who might be threatening Bart
jokes.punchline: Lisa states the mystery story cliché
jokes.explanation: Solid meta-reference to mystery tropes, appropriate for Lisa's literary awareness

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: It's always the butler.

jokes.text: I say we call Matlock. He'll find the culprit. It's probably that evil Gavin MacLeod or George 'Goober' Lindsey.
jokes.setup: Grampa suggesting they call for help
jokes.punchline: Confuses fictional detective with real actors from wholesome TV shows
jokes.explanation: Perfect Grampa moment - mixing fiction/reality while suspecting the most innocent celebrities

dialogue.speaker: Grampa
dialogue.line: We should call for help! Call Matlock!

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Matlock? Grampa, that's just Andy Griffith!

dialogue.speaker: Grampa
dialogue.line: Andy Griffith? I thought he was a real detective!

jokes.text: Grampa, Matlock's not real. Neither are my teeth, but I can still eat corn on the cob... if someone cuts it off and smushes it into a fine paste.
jokes.setup: Bart correcting Grampa about Matlock being fictional
jokes.punchline: Grampa's bizarre analogy about his fake teeth and corn consumption
jokes.explanation: Brilliant Grampa rambling - starts with reasonable point, descends into delightfully specific absurdity

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Matlock is a TV show, Grampa. It's not real.

dialogue.speaker: Grampa
dialogue.line: Not real? Why, I've based my entire legal defense on watching that show! Now you tell me Judge Matlock isn't a real judge?

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: It's just a show.

dialogue.speaker: Grampa
dialogue.line: Well, that's like telling me my teeth aren't real. They're not, but I've gotten a lot of use out of them eating corn on the cob!

jokes.text: The girls are calling you 'fatty-fat fat fat' and Nelson's planning to pull down your pants. But nobody's trying to kill ya.
jokes.setup: Milhouse reporting on school threats to Bart
jokes.punchline: Lists humiliating but harmless pranks, then casually dismisses murder attempts
jokes.explanation: Good contrast between typical kid problems and actual mortal danger

dialogue.speaker: Milhouse
dialogue.line: Bart, I've been keeping a list of all the threats against you. There's the time Jimbo and Dolph pantsed you in front of the girls, when Nelson gave you a noogie that lasted three days, and that incident with the cafeteria mashed potatoes.

dialogue.speaker: Milhouse
dialogue.line: Oh, and I guess there were those few murder attempts, but let's not make a big deal out of it.

jokes.text: All right. This is dedicated to Bart Simpson with a message: 'I am coming to kill you slowly and painfully.'
jokes.setup: Radio dedication setup
jokes.punchline: Death threat delivered in cheerful radio dedication format
jokes.explanation: Excellent juxtaposition of cheerful radio format with sinister message creates perfect absurd comedy

dialogue.speaker: Radio DJ
dialogue.line: We have a special dedication coming in. This one's for Barney Gumble from his wife Chloe. 'Barney, if you don't come home right now, I'm going to kill you.' Well, isn't that romantic! Here's your song.

jokes.text: Bart, I'm going to get you-- some ice cream at the store... since I'm saving so much money on diet cola.
jokes.setup: Threatening opening directed at Bart
jokes.punchline: Innocent completion about ice cream purchase
jokes.explanation: Well-executed misdirection playing on Bart's paranoia

dialogue.speaker: Apu
dialogue.line: Thank you, come again!

jokes.text: Say your prayers, Simpson. Because the schools can't force you like they should.
jokes.setup: Threatening opening to Bart
jokes.punchline: Innocent religious advice
jokes.explanation: Perfect Flanders character moment - turns apparent threat into wholesome religious concern

jokes.text: Maude, these new finger razors make hedge trimming as much fun as sitting through church.
jokes.setup: Ned praising his new gardening tool
jokes.punchline: Compares fun activity to church attendance (which he loves)
jokes.explanation: Brilliant character comedy - Ned thinks church is fun, so this is high praise

dialogue.speaker: Ned Flanders
dialogue.line: Well, I tell you, this new gardening tool is more fun than a barrel of monkeys!

dialogue.speaker: Ned Flanders
dialogue.line: Why, it's almost as enjoyable as attending church on Sunday!

jokes.text: You're going to be my murder victim, Bart... in our school production of Lizzie Borden.
jokes.setup: Threatening statement to Bart
jokes.punchline: About school play casting
jokes.explanation: Good misdirection continuing the pattern, appropriate casting for Martin

dialogue.speaker: Martin Prince
dialogue.line: I'm going to tell Principal Skinner about your behavior, Simpson. And when I do, you'll regret ever crossing me.

dialogue.speaker: Bart Simpson
dialogue.line: Oh yeah? What are you gonna do about it, Martin?

dialogue.speaker: Martin Prince
dialogue.line: Well, I'll have you know that I've already secured the lead role in this year's school play. And there's nothing you can do to stop me from being a star!

dialogue.speaker: Bart Simpson
dialogue.line: A star? Martin, the only thing you're gonna be is a has-been before you even start.

jokes.text: I'm afraid there's no law against mailing threatening letters. I'm pretty sure there is. Ha! The day I take cop lessons from Ma Kettle--
jokes.setup: Police chief stating law incorrectly
jokes.punchline: Marge corrects him, he dismisses her with outdated reference
jokes.explanation: Classic Wiggum incompetence - wrong about the law and sexist dismissal

dialogue.speaker: Chief Wiggum
dialogue.line: According to section 12 of the Springfield municipal code, it's illegal to... uh... exist without a permit.

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: Chief Wiggum, that's not what the law says at all.

dialogue.speaker: Chief Wiggum
dialogue.line: Well, I'm gonna have to ask you to pipe down there, sweetie. This is man's work. Now, as I was saying...

jokes.text: It's also illegal to put squirrels down your pants for the purposes of gambling
jokes.setup: Lou correcting police procedure
jokes.punchline: Cites extremely specific and bizarre illegal activity
jokes.explanation: Brilliantly absurd and specific example that implies a whole backstory of weird Springfield laws

dialogue.speaker: Lou
dialogue.line: Actually, according to statute 247-B, you gotta read him his rights before you even touch him. And you can't search his vehicle unless he's got a bushel of fermented eggs in the trunk.

jokes.text: It's also illegal to put squirrels down your pants for the purposes of gambling.
jokes.setup: Discussion of what's illegal
jokes.punchline: Bizarrely specific gambling law
jokes.explanation: Delightfully absurd and specific law that feels like classic Simpsons random humor

dialogue.speaker: Lou
dialogue.line: Hey, you know what's illegal? Gambling on the outcome of a cockfight.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: What about betting on two blocks of wood floating down the river?

dialogue.speaker: Lou
dialogue.line: That's legal.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Woo-hoo!

jokes.text: [Squirrels Chittering] Boys, knock it off. [All Grumbling]
jokes.setup: Lou just mentioned putting squirrels down pants for gambling
jokes.punchline: Immediate reveal that the cops are currently doing exactly this
jokes.explanation: Perfect immediate payoff showing the police are actively engaged in the bizarre activity just mentioned

dialogue.speaker: Chief Wiggum
dialogue.line: Squirrels down the pants? That's sick! We would never—

dialogue.speaker: Chief Wiggum
dialogue.line: Oh wait, we're doing that right now.

dialogue.speaker: Eddie
dialogue.line: Yeah, I got three in mine.

dialogue.speaker: Lou
dialogue.line: Four here.

jokes.text: Who's someone you've been making irritating phone calls to for years? Linda Lavin? No, someone who didn't deserve it.
jokes.setup: Lisa leading Bart to realize who might be threatening him
jokes.punchline: Bart's random celebrity guess, Lisa's moral judgment
jokes.explanation: Good character moment showing Bart's random associations and Lisa's ethics

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: Bart, think about who would want to threaten you. Who have you wronged recently?

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Uh... Bono?

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: Bart, that's just a random celebrity guess. You need to think logically about this.

jokes.text: Use a pen, Sideshow Bob.
jokes.setup: Sideshow Bob writing with makeshift implement
jokes.punchline: Guard's casual correction implies Bob was using something inappropriate
jokes.explanation: Simple visual gag implying Bob was writing with blood or something equally dramatic

dialogue.speaker: Sideshow Bob
dialogue.line: I shall write my memoirs with this sharpened toothbrush.

dialogue.speaker: Prison Guard
dialogue.line: That's a bone, sir.

jokes.text: Take care, Snake. May the next time we meet... be under more felicitous circumstances. Ga?
jokes.setup: Bob's eloquent farewell to fellow prisoner
jokes.punchline: Snake's confused monosyllabic response
jokes.explanation: Perfect contrast between Bob's verbose intellectualism and Snake's simplicity

dialogue.speaker: Sideshow Bob
dialogue.line: I bid you farewell, my incarcerated compatriot. May your sojourn within these walls be marked by introspection and eventual rehabilitation.

dialogue.speaker: Snake
dialogue.line: Huh?

jokes.text: Sideshow Bob has no decency. He called me 'Chief Piggum.' Oh, now I get it. That's good.
jokes.setup: Wiggum complaining about insult
jokes.punchline: Realizes it's actually clever, appreciates it
jokes.explanation: Perfect Wiggum moment - too slow to be insulted, actually admires the wordplay

dialogue.speaker: Chief Wiggum
dialogue.line: Hey! You can't talk to me like that! That's an insult!

dialogue.speaker: Chief Wiggum
dialogue.line: Wait a minute... that's actually pretty clever.

dialogue.speaker: Chief Wiggum
dialogue.line: I like you. You're alright.

jokes.text: How many people in this court are thinking of killing her right now? Be honest.
jokes.setup: Bob asking about Selma during parole hearing
jokes.punchline: Gets people to raise hands admitting murderous thoughts
jokes.explanation: Dark humor that works because everyone can relate to finding Selma annoying

dialogue.speaker: Sideshow Bob
dialogue.line: I'd like to ask the parole board a question. How many of you have never had a murderous thought?

dialogue.speaker: Parole Board Member 1
dialogue.line: *raises hand*

dialogue.speaker: Sideshow Bob
dialogue.line: Well, I move we kill him. All in favor?

dialogue.speaker: Parole Board Members
dialogue.line: *all raise hands*

jokes.text: Ah, she's always leaving the toilet seat up.
jokes.setup: Person explaining why they want to kill Selma
jokes.punchline: Reverses typical gender complaint about toilet seats
jokes.explanation: Clever reversal of classic domestic complaint, implying Selma has masculine habits

dialogue.speaker: Homer Simpson
dialogue.line: Your Honor, I'd like to explain why I want to kill my wife's sister.

dialogue.speaker: Homer Simpson
dialogue.line: Every morning I wake up, stumble to the bathroom in the dark, and fall in the toilet because Selma leaves the seat down!

jokes.text: We object to the term 'urine-soaked hellhole' when you could have said 'peepee-soaked heckhole.'
jokes.setup: Legal objection to Bob's description of prison
jokes.punchline: Suggests childish euphemisms as alternative
jokes.explanation: Brilliant absurdist legal humor - the alliteration and childish language make it exceptionally quotable

dialogue.speaker: Lawyer
dialogue.line: Objection! My client would prefer not to be described as having "been to prison."

dialogue.speaker: Lawyer
dialogue.line: Perhaps we could say he was "in time out" or "went to the big house for a very long time-out."

jokes.text: No one who speaks German can be an evil man.
jokes.setup: Judge evaluating Bob's German explanation
jokes.punchline: Ignores obvious historical counterexamples
jokes.explanation: Dark historical irony that works perfectly with the German language misdirection

dialogue.speaker: Judge
dialogue.line: Your Honor, I've prepared a detailed explanation of German history and culture.

dialogue.speaker: Bob
dialogue.line: Well, your evidence is compelling, but I'm going to ignore it anyway.

dialogue.speaker: Judge
dialogue.line: Case dismissed.

jokes.text: Oh, really now, that's too much. It's Sideshow Bob!
jokes.setup: Moviegoers complaining about crude movie content
jokes.punchline: More upset by seeing Sideshow Bob than movie vulgarity
jokes.explanation: Good escalation joke - real danger is more shocking than movie content

dialogue.speaker: Movie audience member 1
dialogue.line: This movie has too much profanity and violence!

dialogue.speaker: Movie audience member 2
dialogue.line: Yeah, it's absolutely obscene!

dialogue.speaker: Movie audience member 1
dialogue.line: Wait... is that Sideshow Bob?

dialogue.speaker: All movie audience
dialogue.line: AAHHHHH!

jokes.text: Oh, I'll stay away from your son, all right. Stay away... forever. Oh, no! Wait a minute. That's no good.
jokes.setup: Bob delivering threatening line
jokes.punchline: Realizes his threat doesn't make logical sense, critiques his own villainy
jokes.explanation: Brilliant character moment - Bob's perfectionism extends to his threats, breaks the fourth wall of villainy

dialogue.speaker: Sideshow Bob
dialogue.line: I'll kill you all! I'll kill you till you're dead!

dialogue.speaker: Sideshow Bob
dialogue.line: Wait... that's redundant. If I kill you, you'll already be dead. I really need to work on my threatening monologues.

jokes.text: Wait. I've got a good one now. Marge, say 'Stay away from my son' again. No!
jokes.setup: Bob wanting to retry his threatening response
jokes.punchline: Marge refuses to participate in his theatrical villainy
jokes.explanation: Perfect escalation of Bob's perfectionist villainy and Marge's practical refusal to enable it

dialogue.speaker: Sideshow Bob
dialogue.line: Come now, Mrs. Simpson. Surely you'll reconsider my offer to participate in my diabolical scheme?

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: No, Bob. I'm not going to help you with your theatrical villainy.

dialogue.speaker: Sideshow Bob
dialogue.line: Oh, come on! Just one little monologue?

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: Absolutely not.

jokes.text: And turn and flex. And shake and bounce and turn. And flex and shake and bounce.
jokes.setup: Homer doing exercise routine while securing the house
jokes.punchline: Absurd aerobics routine as home security measure
jokes.explanation: Visual gag playing on Homer's misunderstanding of home security, moderately amusing

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Alright, time for my morning exercise routine and home security check. Gotta stay in shape AND keep the burglars out.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: One! Two! Three! Kick! Now I'm doing lunges while checking the back door. Four! Five! Six! Punch! That's for any intruders AND my love handles!

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Jumping jacks by the windows so criminals know I'm alert and in terrible shape. Seven! Eight! Touching my toes—if I can reach 'em—while locking the front door!

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: This is genius. I'm getting fit, securing the perimeter, AND confusing any burglars who might be watching. Who would rob a house defended by a sweaty, wheezing man in his underwear?

jokes.text: And once a man is in your home, anything you do to him... is nice and legal. Is that so? Oh, Flanders, won't you join me in my kitchen?
jokes.setup: Homer explaining home invasion laws
jokes.punchline: Immediately tries to lure Ned inside with sinister implications
jokes.explanation: Dark humor showing Homer's immediate willingness to test his legal theory on his neighbor

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Hey Ned! Come on in my house! I won't hurt you or nothing!

dialogue.speaker: Ned
dialogue.line: Well, okay Homer!

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Once you're inside, you're trespassing! Hehehehe!

jokes.text: Uh, doesn't work if you invite him. Hidilly-hey!
jokes.setup: Homer's failed attempt to legally trap Ned
jokes.punchline: Ned casually corrects the legal loophole while greeting cheerfully
jokes.explanation: Perfect Ned response - helpful correction followed by oblivious friendliness despite Homer's murderous intent

dialogue.speaker: Ned
dialogue.line: Well hi-diddly-ho there, Homer! Say, that legal trap you set for me? There's a little loophole in subsection B, paragraph 3 that makes the whole thing null and void-a-rooney!

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: D'oh!

jokes.text: Come on! Leave town! No. I'll be your friend. No. Oh, you're mean.
jokes.setup: Wiggum trying to persuade Bob to leave
jokes.punchline: Resorts to childish friendship offer and name-calling
jokes.explanation: Classic Wiggum incompetence - treats dangerous criminal like playground conflict

dialogue.speaker: Chief Wiggum
dialogue.line: Look, Bob, why don't you just come quietly? We can work this out.

dialogue.speaker: Sideshow Bob
dialogue.line: Never, Wiggum! I'll never surrender!

dialogue.speaker: Chief Wiggum
dialogue.line: Okay, okay... how about we be best friends? I'll even let you sit at my lunch table.

dialogue.speaker: Sideshow Bob
dialogue.line: You think you can bribe me with friendship?

dialogue.speaker: Chief Wiggum
dialogue.line: Well, if that doesn't work... you're a big stupid poopyhead!

jokes.text: The following neighborhood residents... will not be killed by me: Ned Flanders... Maude Flanders. Oh, isn't that nice?
jokes.setup: Bob announcing his non-kill list
jokes.punchline: Maude responds as if it's a social courtesy
jokes.explanation: Dark comedy enhanced by Maude treating murder exemption like neighborly politeness

dialogue.speaker: Sideshow Bob
dialogue.line: I have a list of people I'm *not* going to kill.

dialogue.speaker: Maude
dialogue.line: Oh, how thoughtful! May I see it?

jokes.text: Whoo-hoo! Did you hear, Bart? Eh-- Oh.
jokes.setup: Homer celebrating family's safety
jokes.punchline: Realizes Bart wasn't on the safe list
jokes.explanation: Good visual beat showing Homer's delayed realization and parental priorities

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Woohoo! We're all safe! Marge, the kids, Santa's Little Helper, even that stupid cat!

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Wait a minute... Bart wasn't on that list.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: D'oh!

jokes.text: Whoo! I wanna be John Elway.
jokes.setup: Witness protection offering new identities
jokes.punchline: Homer wants to be famous NFL quarterback
jokes.explanation: Classic Homer misunderstanding - thinks witness protection means becoming anyone he wants

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Wait, you're saying I get a new identity? I want to be a famous NFL quarterback!

dialogue.speaker: Federal Agent
dialogue.line: That's... not how witness protection works, Mr. Simpson.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Oh. How about a moderately successful NFL quarterback?

jokes.text: Thanks to Elway's patented last-second magic... the final score of Super Bowl XXX-- Denver, 7 San Francisco, 56.
jokes.setup: Announcer praising Elway's comeback ability
jokes.punchline: Score shows massive loss despite the praise
jokes.explanation: Good sports irony - announcer cliché undermined by terrible score

dialogue.speaker: Sports Announcer
dialogue.line: And that's the kind of comeback ability that makes Elway a legend!

dialogue.speaker: Sports Announcer
dialogue.line: Wait, I'm being told the final score is Denver 3, Kansas City 52.

jokes.text: I'll be Gus, the loveable chimney sweep. Clean as a whistle. Sharp as a thistle. Best in all Westminster. Yeah!
jokes.setup: Bart choosing new identity
jokes.punchline: Elaborate chimney sweep persona with rhyming slogan
jokes.explanation: Charming character moment with Bart's enthusiasm for elaborate fake identity

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: I'm a chimney sweep! Chim-chim-cheree, chim-chim-cheree, chim-chim-cher-oo! I gets all the soot and makes chimneys like new!

jokes.text: Cape Fear, Terror Lake, New Horrorfield, Screamville. Ooh, Ice Creamville. No, Screamville.
jokes.setup: Agent listing ominous town names for relocation
jokes.punchline: Homer misunderstands Screamville as Ice Creamville
jokes.explanation: Good wordplay showing Homer's optimistic misinterpretation of scary place name

dialogue.speaker: FBI Agent
dialogue.line: We can relocate you to Screamville.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Ice Creamville? Why didn't you say so! I'm there!

jokes.text: When I say, 'Hello, Mr. Thompson,' you'll say, 'Hi.' Check. Hello, Mr. Thompson.
jokes.setup: Agent explaining simple identity protocol to Homer
jokes.punchline: Homer fails immediately by not responding
jokes.explanation: Classic Homer incompetence - can't handle the simplest instruction

dialogue.speaker: FBI Agent
dialogue.line: Now Homer, for security purposes, when I call you on the phone, I'll say 'The package is in the mail.' You respond with 'The mailman never rings twice.'

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Okay.

dialogue.speaker: FBI Agent
dialogue.line: Let's practice. The package is in the mail.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: What?

jokes.text: Remember now-- your name is Homer Thompson. I gotcha. Hello, Mr. Thompson.
jokes.setup: Agent reminding Homer of his new name
jokes.punchline: Homer still doesn't respond when addressed
jokes.explanation: Good escalation of Homer's inability to grasp simple concept

dialogue.speaker: FBI Agent
dialogue.line: Remember, your new name is Homer Thompson.

dialogue.speaker: FBI Agent
dialogue.line: Homer Thompson!

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Huh? What?

jokes.text: Hello, Mr. Thompson. I think he's talking to you.
jokes.setup: Third attempt to get Homer to respond to his fake name
jokes.punchline: Marge has to prompt Homer
jokes.explanation: Solid conclusion to running gag, Marge's exasperation adds to the humor

dialogue.speaker: FBI Agent
dialogue.line: Mr. Hernandez? Mr. Hernandez?

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: Homer, that's you.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Oh! Yeah?

jokes.text: Hey, look! The F.B.I. Light Opera Society Sings the Complete Gilbert and Sullivan.
jokes.setup: Family getting in witness protection car
jokes.punchline: FBI has elaborate musical theater collection
jokes.explanation: Delightfully absurd detail about FBI culture, sets up the musical elements to come

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: So we're really doing this? Witness protection?

dialogue.speaker: FBI Agent
dialogue.line: That's correct. You'll be relocated immediately. Now, before we go, I should mention—we have an elaborate musical theater collection back at headquarters.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Musical theater? Like... Broadway?

dialogue.speaker: FBI Agent
dialogue.line: Everything from Oklahoma! to the latest productions. We even have a fully equipped performance space.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Why would the FBI need that?

dialogue.speaker: FBI Agent
dialogue.line: Morale. It's important work.

jokes.text: Eh, lousy speed bumps.
jokes.setup: Car going over bumps in road
jokes.punchline: Homer doesn't realize he's running over Sideshow Bob
jokes.explanation: Excellent physical comedy with perfect understated delivery - visual and dialogue work together perfectly

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Woohoo! Bumpy road!

dialogue.speaker: Sideshow Bob
dialogue.line: Ow! Ow! Ow! Ow!

jokes.text: Hey, kids, wanna drive through that cactus patch? Yeah! Yeah! No! Whoop, two against one.
jokes.setup: Homer asking kids about driving route
jokes.punchline: Democratic decision leads to more torture for hidden Sideshow Bob
jokes.explanation: Great escalation of Bob's suffering with family democracy making it worse

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Kids, we need to decide on a route home. Should we take the highway or go through the forest?

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: The forest!

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: Actually, I think the highway would be more efficient.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: We'll take a vote. All in favor of the forest?

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Aye!

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: The forest it is!

dialogue.speaker: Sideshow Bob
dialogue.line: No! Not the forest! Anything but the forest!

jokes.text: You know, the great thing is, if you don't like your neighbors... you can just pull up the anchor and sail someplace else.
jokes.setup: Homer explaining benefits of houseboat living
jokes.punchline: Ironic since they're hiding from a specific threat who will follow them
jokes.explanation: Good ironic setup for Bob's inevitable appearance on the boat

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: A houseboat? It's the perfect solution! We can just float away from all our problems.

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: Homer, we're hiding from the IRS. They have boats too.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: D'oh!

jokes.text: Hello! Hello! You have my pills! Hello? I'm cold, and there are wolves after me.
jokes.setup: Grampa left behind after family's hasty departure
jokes.punchline: Increasingly desperate and absurd situation
jokes.explanation: Good escalating absurdity showing consequences of Homer's poor planning

dialogue.speaker: Grampa Simpson
dialogue.line: Wait, wait! Don't leave me! I'm still here! Hello? Hello?!

dialogue.speaker: Grampa Simpson
dialogue.line: I guess I'll just eat this whole wheel of cheese myself.

dialogue.speaker: Grampa Simpson
dialogue.line: Now I'm talking to the potted plant. How ya doin', buddy?

dialogue.speaker: Grampa Simpson
dialogue.line: Oh, that's it! I'm calling the cops! No wait, I can't remember the number. Is it 911 or 411?

dialogue.speaker: Grampa Simpson
dialogue.line: I'm gonna go live with the pigeons now. At least they appreciate an old man!

jokes.text: Surely there's no harm... in laying in the middle of a public street.
jokes.setup: Bob hiding under the car, now exposed in street
jokes.punchline: Rationalizes obviously dangerous position
jokes.explanation: Good character moment showing Bob's educated rationalization of absurd situation

dialogue.speaker: Sideshow Bob
dialogue.line: Oh, hello. I was just, uh, checking the undercarriage for rust.

jokes.text: Roman numeral three: Surprise boy in bed-- and, uh, disembowel him. No, I don't like that 'bowel' in there. Gut him.
jokes.setup: Bob planning Bart's murder methodically
jokes.punchline: Gets distracted by word choice aesthetics
jokes.explanation: Perfect Sideshow Bob moment - his intellectual pretensions extend even to murder planning, brilliant character comedy

dialogue.speaker: Sideshow Bob
dialogue.line: I shall kill Bart Simpson with a gun... no, too pedestrian. A knife? Overdone. Perhaps poison? No, no... I'll use a rake!

dialogue.speaker: Sideshow Bob
dialogue.line: Wait, 'rake'... such an ugly word. Doesn't roll off the tongue. Let me reconsider. A *scythe*? Yes, much more poetic, much more... theatrical.

dialogue.speaker: Sideshow Bob
dialogue.line: Though 'scythe' does have that unfortunate hissing sound... Perhaps 'guillotine'? No, I've used that before. What about 'elaborate contraption of gears and pulleys'?

dialogue.speaker: Sideshow Bob
dialogue.line: Hmm, too many syllables. The perfect murder requires the perfect word. I simply cannot proceed until I find the ideal descriptor for Bart's demise!

jokes.text: Bart, you want some brownie before you go to bed? Come on. Let me cut you a brownie while they're still hot.
jokes.setup: Homer offering Bart food with knife
jokes.punchline: Innocent gesture sounds threatening given context
jokes.explanation: Good dramatic irony - Homer's innocent actions seem threatening to paranoid Bart

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Hey Bart, want some candy? I got it right here on this knife.

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Dad, that's the creepiest thing you've ever said.

jokes.text: Dad, I'm kind of edgy right now. I'd appreciate you not coming in my room screaming and brandishing a butcher knife.
jokes.setup: Bart responding to Homer's innocent brownie offer
jokes.punchline: Calmly requests father not terrorize him
jokes.explanation: Excellent deadpan delivery of very reasonable request given the circumstances

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Dad, please stop trying to poison me.

jokes.text: Bart, do you want to see my new chainsaw and hockey mask?
jokes.setup: Homer trying to be considerate after previous scare
jokes.punchline: Immediately does something even more terrifying
jokes.explanation: Perfect escalation - Homer learns wrong lesson and makes things worse

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: I'll be more careful from now on.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: I'm gonna go practice my chainsaw juggling in the living room!

jokes.text: Oh, no, Dad's been drugged. No, he hasn't.
jokes.setup: Bart seeing Homer unconscious
jokes.punchline: Bob clarifies Homer is just naturally that way
jokes.explanation: Good character joke about Homer's natural state being indistinguishable from being drugged

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Dad! Dad, wake up!

dialogue.speaker: Sideshow Bob
dialogue.line: Don't worry, boy. Your father isn't dead. He's just naturally like that.

jokes.text: Well, you have such a beautiful voice. Guilty as charged. Anyway, I was wondering if you could sing the entire score of the H.M.S. Pinafore.
jokes.setup: Bart making final request before death
jokes.punchline: Asks for lengthy musical performance, Bob accepts compliment
jokes.explanation: Brilliant setup exploiting Bob's vanity and musical theater knowledge to buy time

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Mr. Bob, before you kill me, I have one last request.

dialogue.speaker: Sideshow Bob
dialogue.line: What is it, Bart?

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: I'd like to hear you sing that Gilbert and Sullivan number one more time.

dialogue.speaker: Sideshow Bob
dialogue.line: Why, Bart! I'm touched! You DO appreciate my talents!

dialogue.speaker: Sideshow Bob
dialogue.line: I'd be delighted to perform 'I Am the Very Model of a Modern Major-General' for you.

jokes.text: Very well, Bart. I shall send you to heaven before I send you to hell.
jokes.setup: Bob agreeing to sing before killing Bart
jokes.punchline: Pretentious way of saying he'll sing beautifully then murder
jokes.explanation: Perfect Bob pomposity - turns simple agreement into theatrical declaration

dialogue.speaker: Sideshow Bob
dialogue.line: I shall endeavor to regale you with a mellifluous rendition of my vocal prowess before I dispatch young Bart to the netherworld.

jokes.text: Ah, yeah. It's a good thing you drifted by this brothel.
jokes.setup: Police arriving to arrest Sideshow Bob
jokes.punchline: Casual mention of brothel location
jokes.explanation: Random absurd detail about the rescue location, mild shock humor

dialogue.speaker: Chief Wiggum
dialogue.line: Alright, Sideshow Bob, you're under arrest.

dialogue.speaker: Sideshow Bob
dialogue.line: You'll never take me alive, Chief!

dialogue.speaker: Chief Wiggum
dialogue.line: We know where you live - we've been there plenty of times. Great buffet.

jokes.text: I knew I had to buy some time... so I asked him to sing the score from the H.M.S. Pinafore. Ooh, a plan fiendishly clever in its intricacies.
jokes.setup: Bart explaining his survival strategy
jokes.punchline: Bob compliments the plan even though it defeated him
jokes.explanation: Perfect Bob character moment - his intellectual appreciation overrides his anger at being outsmarted

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: If you're gonna survive against a supervillain, you gotta think like one.

dialogue.speaker: Sideshow Bob
dialogue.line: That's actually a pretty clever strategy, boy. I'm impressed, even though it completely foiled my plans for revenge.

jokes.text: Take him away, boys. Hey, I'm the chief here. Bake him away, toys.
jokes.setup: Lou giving arrest order
jokes.punchline: Wiggum asserts authority with garbled command
jokes.explanation: Classic Wiggum malapropism that became iconic - perfect character moment with incredible quotability

dialogue.speaker: Lou
dialogue.line: Chief, we got him surrounded!

dialogue.speaker: Chief Wiggum
dialogue.line: Alright Lou, let's do this by the book. Ready? One, two, three... CHARGE!

jokes.text: Look what happened without my pills!
jokes.setup: Family returning home to find Grampa
jokes.punchline: Grampa has apparently transformed without medication
jokes.explanation: Visual payoff to earlier setup about Grampa being left behind with his medication

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Dad? What happened to you?

dialogue.speaker: Grampa
dialogue.line: Well, I ran out of my pills, so I figured I'd just see what happens.

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: Oh my God, you're covered in... is that mud?

dialogue.speaker: Grampa
dialogue.line: And leaves. And I think a small woodland creature. But I feel great!

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Dude, you're like a nature documentary.

jokes.text: Not so fast. I wanna court this fair, young maiden. There's somethin' you should know about me. I've got Steve and Eydie tickets. I'm all yours!
jokes.setup: Jasper pursuing romantic interest
jokes.punchline: Grampa's revelation about concert tickets wins him over
jokes.explanation: Good character comedy showing elderly romance priorities and period-appropriate entertainment references

dialogue.speaker: Jasper
dialogue.line: I got you concert tickets!

dialogue.speaker: Grampa
dialogue.line: Well, I got you something better — I got you concert tickets too, but mine are from 1974!

tmdb_overview: The Simpsons go incognito to avoid the newly paroled Sideshow Bob, who's out to get Bart.
insight: Parody mastery: Psycho riff escalates to 62 jokes across 22 minutes at elite density.
